summary: Jeff Atwood argues that traditional username/password authentication is fundamentally broken, with most users defaulting to weak passwords and effectively using their email as a global password. He examines PayPal's adoption of two-factor authentication via keyfobs, explaining the three pillars of security: what you have, what you know, and what you are. While acknowledging Bruce Schneier's warnings about man-in-the-middle and trojan attacks that can defeat two-factor authentication, Atwood concludes that any improvement over the current password-only system is worthwhile despite not being a complete solution.
key_points: Traditional passwords are broken - most users choose weak passwords like 'password1' and rely on password reset emails, making email accounts effectively their global password
Two-factor authentication combines 'something you know' (password) with 'something you have' (keyfob/smart card) to dramatically increase security
Smart cards with embedded microprocessors can replace passwords entirely with randomly generated 64-character strings, but only work for local authentication
Two-factor authentication remains vulnerable to real-time phishing (man-in-the-middle) and trojan attacks on compromised systems
Despite its limitations, two-factor authentication is a worthwhile improvement over the current insecure password-only approach
money_quotes: We used to quip that 'password' is the most common password. Now it's 'password1.' Who said users haven't learned anything about security?
My email account is my global password.
All security is based on three things: What you have, What you know, What you are.
Switching to passphrases helps, but is a band-aid at best.
I'm not sure any security measures can work on a compromised system with trojan keyloggers and screenloggers installed.
Anything that moves the security bar beyond the hopelessly insecure and ineffective username/password combos we're currently stuck with is a welcome change.
key_insight: Two-factor authentication isn't perfect, but the username/password system is so fundamentally broken that any meaningful improvement in authentication security is worth pursuing.
